transcript:
You're watching 600 identical particles start as a dense, scattered swarm and slowly consolidate into a single comet streaking off the bottom of the screen. What looks like one dramatic shift is actually hundreds of small decisions compounding.

Here's the mechanism: each particle has an "affinities" map — literally a memory of who it's been near. In accumulate affinities, proximity doesn't just trigger attraction, it *builds* it, gain accumulating with repeated closeness and decaying slowly when a neighbor drifts away. Nothing in the code says "form one cluster." That outcome comes from a second detail, in the color definitions: particles near a "beloved" glow warm with "affinityWarmth," while isolated ones cool toward "lonelinessCool." Once a group starts winning, warmth reinforces proximity, which reinforces warmth — a feedback loop the artist set in motion but didn't design the ending for.

So what you're seeing at 00:35, when the swarm splits into one bright body and a field of abandoned stragglers, isn't a scripted climax. It's memory outcompeting randomness — the system finding a preference nobody wrote down.
